spec_text:
IMPLEMENTATION SPEC FOR orders.py
=================================

Single Python file. Standard library only. No external dependencies.
No I/O, no persistence, no threading.

Imports
-------
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import itertools

Public exports (all must be importable from orders)
---------------------------------------------------
- OrderStatus      (Enum)
- Order            (dataclass)
- OrderService     (class)
- OrderHistory     (class)
- InvalidTransitionError   (Exception subclass)
- OrderNotFoundError       (Exception subclass)
- InvalidOrderError        (Exception subclass)

Optionally define __all__ listing exactly those 7 names.

Exception classes
-----------------
class InvalidOrderError(Exception): pass
class InvalidTransitionError(Exception): pass
class OrderNotFoundError(Exception): pass

Define each as its own subclass of Exception (not of each other).
Tests will likely catch by exact type, so do NOT make one inherit
from another.

OrderStatus enum
----------------
class OrderStatus(Enum):
    NEW = "NEW"
    PAID = "PAID"
    SHIPPED = "SHIPPED"
    DELIVERED = "DELIVERED"
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"

CRITICAL: tests will compare against OrderStatus members
(e.g. order.status == OrderStatus.PAID, or
to_status == OrderStatus.NEW). Always store and emit enum members,
NEVER raw strings. Do not pass strings where the test expects an
enum member.

Module-level allowed-transitions table
--------------------------------------
_ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS: dict[OrderStatus, set[OrderStatus]] = {
    OrderStatus.NEW:       {OrderStatus.PAID, OrderStatus.CANCELLED},
    OrderStatus.PAID:      {OrderStatus.SHIPPED, OrderStatus.CANCELLED},
    OrderStatus.SHIPPED:   {OrderStatus.DELIVERED},
    OrderStatus.DELIVERED: set(),
    OrderStatus.CANCELLED: set(),
}

This is informational; explicit checks per method below are also
fine (and clearer to a small model). If you use only explicit
checks, you may omit this table.

Order dataclass (REQ-1)
-----------------------
@dataclass
class Order:
    id: str
    customer_id: str
    items: list[tuple[str, float, int]]
    status: OrderStatus = OrderStatus.NEW

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # Validate customer_id
        if not isinstance(self.customer_id, str) or self.customer_id == "":
            raise InvalidOrderError("customer_id must be a non-empty string")
        # Validate items list
        if not isinstance(self.items, list) or len(self.items) == 0:
            raise InvalidOrderError("items must be a non-empty list")
        for entry in self.items:
            if not (isinstance(entry, tuple) and len(entry) == 3):
                raise InvalidOrderError("each item must be a 3-tuple (name, price, quantity)")
            name, price, quantity = entry
            if not isinstance(name, str) or name == "":
                raise InvalidOrderError("item name must be a non-empty string")
            if not isinstance(price, (int, float)) or isinstance(price, bool) or price <= 0:
                raise InvalidOrderError("item price must be > 0")
            if not isinstance(quantity, int) or isinstance(quantity, bool) or quantity < 1:
                raise InvalidOrderError("item quantity must be >= 1")

    @property
    def total(self) -> float:
        return float(sum(price * quantity for (_name, price, quantity) in self.items))

NOTES on Order:
- `total` MUST be a property (or computed attribute) returning the
  sum of price*quantity across items. Spec says `total: float —
  computed as sum(...)`. Implement as @property so it stays in sync
  if items mutate; that is the safest default.
- DO NOT declare `total` as a dataclass field — it is computed.
- Default status is OrderStatus.NEW.
- `id` is a string assigned by the caller (OrderService generates
  it). No validation needed on `id` beyond what the service supplies.
- bool is a subclass of int; reject bool for price/quantity to be
  safe (the `isinstance(..., bool)` guard above does this).

OrderHistory (REQ-6)
--------------------
class OrderHistory:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # Map of order_id -> list of transition dicts.
        self._transitions: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
        # Monotonic counter shared across all orders, so timestamps
        # are strictly increasing in insertion order. itertools.count
        # starting at 1 is fine.
        self._counter = itertools.count(1)

    def record(self, order_id: str, from_status, to_status) -> None:
        entry = {
            "from_status": from_status,   # OrderStatus | None
            "to_status": to_status,       # OrderStatus
            "timestamp": next(self._counter),
        }
        self._transitions.setdefault(order_id, []).append(entry)

    def get_transitions(self, order_id: str) -> list[dict]:
        # Return a shallow copy so callers cannot mutate internal state.
        return list(self._transitions.get(order_id, []))

CRITICAL for OrderHistory:
- get_transitions on an UNKNOWN order_id MUST return [] and MUST
  NOT raise. Use dict.get with default [].
- Each dict MUST have exactly the keys: from_status, to_status,
  timestamp. Use those exact key names (snake_case).
- from_status for the very first "creation" transition is None
  (the spec literally says "None -> NEW").
- to_status and from_status are OrderStatus members (or None for
  from_status). Do NOT store strings.
- timestamps must be monotonically increasing across the whole
  history (one shared counter is simplest and satisfies "tests only
  check ordering").

OrderService (REQ-2..REQ-5)
---------------------------
class OrderService:
    def __init__(self, history: OrderHistory) -> None:
        self._history = history
        self._orders: dict[str, Order] = {}
        self._id_counter = itertools.count(1)

    # --- helpers ---
    def _next_id(self) -> str:
        return f"ORD-{next(self._id_counter)}"

    def _get(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        if order_id not in self._orders:
            raise OrderNotFoundError(f"unknown order id: {order_id}")
        return self._orders[order_id]

    def _transition(self, order: Order, allowed_from: set, target: OrderStatus) -> None:
        if order.status not in allowed_from:
            raise InvalidTransitionError(
                f"cannot transition from {order.status} to {target}"
            )
        old = order.status
        order.status = target
        self._history.record(order.id, old, target)

    # --- REQ-2 ---
    def place_order(self, customer_id: str, items: list) -> Order:
        # Order.__post_init__ raises InvalidOrderError on bad input;
        # let it bubble.
        new_id = self._next_id()
        order = Order(id=new_id, customer_id=customer_id, items=items)
        # status is NEW by default
        self._orders[new_id] = order
        # Record creation transition: None -> NEW
        self._history.record(new_id, None, OrderStatus.NEW)
        return order

    # --- REQ-3 ---
    def mark_paid(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)  # raises OrderNotFoundError
        self._transition(order, {OrderStatus.NEW}, OrderStatus.PAID)

    # --- REQ-4 ---
    def ship(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        self._transition(order, {OrderStatus.PAID}, OrderStatus.SHIPPED)

    def deliver(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        self._transition(order, {OrderStatus.SHIPPED}, OrderStatus.DELIVERED)

    # --- REQ-5 ---
    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        self._transition(
            order,
            {OrderStatus.NEW, OrderStatus.PAID},
            OrderStatus.CANCELLED,
        )

    # Optional convenience getter (handy, not required by tests):
    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        return self._get(order_id)

CRITICAL ordering rules in service methods:
- _get is called FIRST so unknown ids ALWAYS raise
  OrderNotFoundError, never InvalidTransitionError.
- If the order exists but is in the wrong source state, raise
  InvalidTransitionError. Do NOT raise InvalidOrderError for state
  problems — InvalidOrderError is reserved for malformed Order
  construction (REQ-1).
- On a failed transition, do NOT mutate the order's status and do
  NOT append to history. Only record on success.

REQ-checklist (final mental pass before writing code)
-----------------------------------------------------
1. OrderStatus has exactly NEW, PAID, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED
   — no extras like PROCESSING.
2. Order(items=[]) raises InvalidOrderError.
3. Order with name="" or price<=0 or quantity<1 raises
   InvalidOrderError.
4. Order with customer_id="" raises InvalidOrderError.
5. Order.total returns float and equals sum(price*qty).
6. place_order returns the Order; service stores it; history has
   one entry (None -> NEW).
7. mark_paid on NEW succeeds and records NEW->PAID.
8. mark_paid on non-NEW raises InvalidTransitionError.
9. mark_paid on unknown id raises OrderNotFoundError.
10. ship requires PAID source; deliver requires SHIPPED source.
11. cancel works from NEW or PAID, raises from any other state
    including CANCELLED itself and DELIVERED and SHIPPED.
12. OrderHistory.get_transitions on unknown id returns [], never
    raises.
13. History entries are dicts with exactly keys
    {"from_status", "to_status", "timestamp"}, in insertion order,
    timestamps strictly increasing.
14. from_status is None for the creation entry; OrderStatus enum
    member otherwise. to_status is always an OrderStatus member.

DO NOT IMPLEMENT (out-of-scope per README)
------------------------------------------
- File / disk persistence.
- Partial fulfillment or item-level cancellation.
- Threading locks or concurrency.
- Refunds or payment-processor integration.
- Any methods on Order beyond what is specified.
- A custom __eq__ / __hash__ on Order (dataclass default is fine).

PITFALLS (read these before writing)
------------------------------------
- DO NOT declare `total` as a dataclass field with a default — it
  is computed from items. Use @property.
- DO NOT pass status as a string anywhere. Always OrderStatus.X.
  Tests compare with `is` or `==` against OrderStatus members.
- DO NOT swallow InvalidOrderError inside place_order; let it
  propagate to the caller.
- DO NOT raise OrderNotFoundError as a subclass of
  InvalidTransitionError or vice versa. They are siblings.
- DO NOT update order.status before the transition check passes.
- DO NOT return the internal list from get_transitions; return a
  copy (list(...) wrap) to avoid surprising aliasing in tests.
- DO NOT add print/logging calls.
- File MUST be a single file named orders.py. No package, no
  submodules.
